cards.title: Phase 2 Decisions

children.title: Data Model Changes
Drop position field
children.body: Array index IS the display order. recompact() goes away. addItem/moveItem simplify.

children.title: Drop ordering field
children.body: No more custom/alpha-asc/alpha-desc on containers. getOrderedChildren() just returns items as-is. Agent sorts on demand via move operations.

children.title: Rename notes to body
children.body: Generic free-form content field. Can store bug descriptions, ideas, agent instructions, format guidance. Parent body can instruct agent on how to interpret children.

children.title: Simplified item schema
children.body: {id, title, created, archived, body, children[]}
These affect Phase 1 code. Must be done before or alongside Phase 2 features.

children.title: View System
Flat render array
children.body: Each item: {id, title, depth, descendantCount, hasNotes, notesPreview, created, archived}. Pre-walked to requested depth. Agent indents by depth and appends (N).

children.title: Depth model
children.body: No flag = item + direct children. --depth N = N levels of full details. --depth 0 = infinite/no cutoff. Items at cutoff show (N) active descendant count.

children.title: Breadcrumbs on focus
children.body: Focused subtree shows item as root + ancestry breadcrumbs. Presentation at Claude's discretion. Doesn't have to be inline with items.

children.title: Archived excluded by default
children.body: Active views hide archived. Flag like --include-archived or --archived-only to see them.
get is the universal view command. Flat render array. Breadcrumbs for focus.

children.title: Archive Behavior
Cascade archive
children.body: Archiving a parent archives all descendants. The whole subtree disappears from active view.

children.title: Cascade unarchive
children.body: Symmetrical. Unarchiving restores the whole subtree.

children.title: Active-only descendant counts
children.body: If bugs has 5 children and 2 archived, count shows (3) not (5). Consistent with archived=hidden.

children.title: Archived tree position
children.body: When viewing archived items, show in original tree structure, not flattened.
Cascade archive/unarchive. Active-only counts. Original tree position for archived views.

children.title: CLI Command Surface
get is universal view
children.body: get [id] [--depth N] covers all read operations. No separate render/list/dump/view commands.

children.title: Aliases
children.body: list = get (no args). dump = get --depth 0. children = get <id>. path = get with breadcrumbs only. Same code path, DRY.

children.title: New: archive/unarchive
children.body: archive <id> and unarchive <id>. Both cascade to descendants.

children.title: Stateless CLI
children.body: No persistent working directory. Agent holds context conversationally. Human steers with natural language, agent translates to IDs.
Simplify to get as universal view. Aliases for list/dump/children. New archive/unarchive commands.

children.title: Philosophy
No search command
children.body: Agent ingests tree via get --depth 0, searches in memory. Burrow is a structured dataset + view renderer, not a query engine.

children.title: Same interface, different strengths
children.body: Human and agent use identical CLI. Agent advantage: bulk ingest full state, use direct ID commands. Human advantage: natural language via agent.

children.title: Body as agent instructions
children.body: Parent body can describe schema/purpose of children. Self-documenting tree structures. E.g. 'Store bugs here. Title=description. Body=repro steps.'

children.title: No artificial size limits
children.body: Limit is user's machine. Guardrails warn but don't block. Agent optimizes token consumption with smart depth queries. Can cache shortcuts in its own context.
No search command. Agent ingests + searches in memory. Same interface for humans and agents. Body field as self-documenting structure.

cards.body: Decisions from discuss-phase session 2026-03-07. Follow up on these during planning and execution.

cards.title: v1 Remaining Work
cards.body: Phases 3 and 4. Prove the value — make it work, not bulletproof.

children.title: Phase 4: CLI Pretty-Print Rendering
children.body: Bake tree visualization into the tool. Default = pretty-printed tree. --json flag for structured data. Faster, consistent, no token waste.

children.title: Move --at flag for reordering
children.body:
Support --at <position> on move command. Without --to, reorders within current parent. With --to, places at specific position in destination. Both flags optional independently.

Default: if --at is not specified, card is added last (appended to end of children array). This matches current behavior for add and move.

children.title: dump command should always use --full --depth 0
children.body: dump is an alias for read with --full --depth 0 (full tree, full bodies). It's the 'show me everything' command. Currently dump does not pass --full by default — this card tracks adding that.

children.title: get --depth should expand nested children in pretty-print
children.body: Currently --depth flag doesn't affect pretty-print output — always shows only immediate children. Should render nested tree up to the specified depth level with indentation.

children.title: archived-only should still show [archived] tag
children.body: When using --archived-only, cards don't show the [archived] tag. For consistency, the tag should always appear on archived cards regardless of filter mode.

children.title: Phase 3: Agent Interface
children.body: Workflow file + slash commands. /gsd:burrow for natural language, shortcuts for common ops. Depends on phase 2 (complete).

children.title: Render: move count after title, replace dot with ellipsis
children.body: Change tree line format. Move (N) descendant count right after title (hide when 0). Replace • body indicator with … ellipsis after count. Order of indicators: title → count → ellipsis → [archived] → time. Five combos: 'Title (6) …' (count+body), 'Title (1)' (count only), 'Title …' (body only), 'Title' (neither), 'Title (25) … [archived]' (all indicators).

children.title: Agent: translate natural language positions to 0-based --at index
children.body: The --at flag uses 0-based indexing (CS standard). Keep it that way. When users say 'move to position 3' or 'make it third', the agent should translate to --at 2. The workflow layer handles the human-friendly ↔ programmatic mapping, not the CLI.

cards.title: --help flag design
cards.body:
When the user runs 'npx create-burrow --help', print this:

There's a hole in the ground next to your project.
Not a real one — a metaphysical one.

  npx create-burrow [path]    dig one here (default: .)
  npx create-burrow --yes     dig without asking questions
  npx create-burrow --uninstall   fill it back in (your cards survive)
  npx create-burrow --help    you are here

First time? Just run: npx create-burrow

The branding writes itself. The help text IS the onboarding — it tells you what burrow is (a hole), what it does (you throw thoughts in), and how to use it (three commands). No man page, no --verbose, no walls of flags. A poem that happens to be documentation.

children.title: Positioning
children.body: Burrow fills the niche between too little and too much. Simple by design. Not a database, not a bare file. Just enough structure for agents to stay organized without overhead.

children.title: Security & Privacy Awareness
children.body: The burrow file lives in the repo — anything stored in cards is in git history forever. The agent should: (1) never put API keys, secrets, or sensitive data in cards, (2) warn users if card content looks sensitive, (3) treat cards as public-by-default. Same risk as hardcoding an API key — users should know, and the agent should actively guard against it.

cards.title: Mock
cards.body: Demo data for showcasing nested tree rendering at various depths.

children.title: Auth API
children.body: JWT + refresh tokens, rate limiting

children.title: Token Refresh Flow
children.body: Handle expired tokens gracefully

children.title: Silent Refresh
children.body: Background token renewal without UI flash

children.title: Retry Strategy
children.body: Exponential backoff on 401

children.title: Widget Grid
children.body: Drag-and-drop layout, responsive

children.title: Settings Page
children.body: User prefs, theme, notifications

children.title: GitHub Actions
children.body: Build, test, deploy pipeline

cards.title: Backlog
cards.body: Future work items and ideas. Not tied to a specific version or milestone.

children.title: Concurrency & Multi-Agent
children.body: File locking, stale lock detection, multi-agent safety. Two Claude Code sessions on the same burrow shouldn't lose data.

children.title: Lock file semaphore
children.body: cards.json.lock — check before writing, create, write, delete. Prevents concurrent write conflicts.

children.title: Stale lock cleanup
children.body: Handle crashed processes that left a lock file behind. Timeout or PID check.

children.title: Multi-agent write safety
children.body: Multiple agents operating on the same burrow without silent data loss. The real problem: read-modify-write race condition.

children.title: Multi-file scaling
children.body: Split large trees across separate files. For when a single cards.json gets too big. Don't compromise simplicity for this.

children.title: Plugin packaging
children.body: npm package for standalone installation. Zero deps = no build step needed. Core engine (mongoose, warren, render, core) needs zero changes — just packaging plumbing. Key tasks: (1) Create package.json with bin entry pointing to burrow-tools.cjs, (2) Update init.cjs to resolve paths from installed package location instead of symlinks, (3) Update CLAUDE.md snippet to use 'burrow' or 'npx burrow' instead of 'node .claude/burrow/burrow-tools.cjs', (4) Bundle slash command .md files and workflow so init can copy them from the package. Design decision: support both global install (npm i -g) and npx. Estimated effort: a few hours.

children.title: GSD integration
children.body:
## Vision

Burrow as the planning backbone — replace GSD's .planning/ markdown files with structured, nestable, archivable cards.

Milestone = card. Phase = child card. Research, planning, execution = child cards under the phase. Bodies hold full markdown. Archive a milestone when done — one operation, entire tree preserved but hidden.

## Why

Markdown file constellations drift. ROADMAP.md falls out of sync with STATE.md, phase directories accumulate stale files. Burrow's single-tree structure resists this by design — one source of truth, queryable, archivable.

## Guiding Principle

Keep Burrow simple. If its existing primitives (cards, nesting, archive, bodies) naturally solve planning problems, great. Do NOT add adapter layers, export/import commands, or multi-file complexity just to force-fit GSD integration. GSD should come to Burrow, not the other way around.

## What's Actually Worth Building

- `burrow index` command — cheap tree loading (titles/IDs only, no bodies). Benefits all use cases, not just GSD. ~70 lines.
- Everything else stays as research until Burrow naturally grows into it.

## Research

See child cards under Research for detailed analysis of token costs, selective loading, archive storage, GSD compatibility, migration paths, and multi-file split designs. These are reference material, not a roadmap.

children.title: Token cost: cards.json vs GSD file-per-artifact
children.body:
## Token Cost Analysis: cards.json vs GSD File-Per-Artifact

### Current State Snapshot

#### cards.json (Burrow)
- **File size**: 121,159 bytes / 1,302 lines
- **Approx tokens**: ~30,290 tokens
- **Cards**: 9 root cards, 0 children (flat structure currently)
- **Avg card size**: 13,417 bytes (~3,354 tokens)
- **Largest card**: "v1.2 Packaging & Distribution" at 71,083 bytes (~17,771 tokens) — a single card consuming 59% of the file

#### GSD Planning Files
- **Total .planning/ directory**: 807,291 bytes across 105 markdown files
- **Approx tokens (full load)**: ~201,823 tokens

#### GSD Core Files (loaded every session)
| File | Bytes | ~Tokens |
|------|-------|---------|
| PROJECT.md | 11,437 | 2,859 |
| ROADMAP.md | 1,730 | 433 |
| STATE.md | 1,795 | 449 |
| MILESTONES.md | 2,082 | 521 |
| RETROSPECTIVE.md | 6,544 | 1,636 |
| **Subtotal** | **23,588** | **5,897** |

#### GSD Codebase Files (loaded for dev work)
- Total: 15,282 bytes (~3,821 tokens)

#### GSD Per-Phase (loaded only when working on that phase)
- Phase 08 example: 75,747 bytes (~18,937 tokens) across 9 files
- Typical session loads: PLAN + SUMMARY for current sub-phase ≈ 10,000–16,000 bytes (~2,500–4,000 tokens)

### Selective Loading Advantage

**GSD typical session cost:**
- Core files: ~5,897 tokens (always)
- Current phase plan + summary: ~4,000 tokens (selective)
- **Total: ~10,000 tokens per session**

**Burrow current session cost:**
- Full cards.json: ~30,290 tokens (always, all-or-nothing)
- **3x more expensive than GSD right now**

### Growth Projections

Current state: 9 cards over ~1.3 milestones of work. Average card = 13,417 bytes, but the median is much lower (~3,000 bytes). The v1.2 card is an outlier at 71KB.

Using **median card size (~3,000 bytes)** and estimating ~15–25 cards per milestone:

| Milestones | Est. Cards | cards.json Size | ~Tokens | GSD Equivalent Session Cost |
|------------|-----------|----------------|---------|----------------------------|
| Current (1.3) | 9 | 121 KB | 30K | ~10K (selective) |
| 5 | 50–75 | 150–225 KB | 38K–56K | ~10K (selective) |
| 10 | 100–150 | 300–450 KB | 75K–113K | ~12K (selective) |
| 20 | 200–300 | 600–900 KB | 150K–225K | ~15K (selective) |

Note: With **mean card size (13,417 bytes)**, projections would be 3–4x higher, but the mean is skewed by the 71KB outlier.

### Crossover / Danger Points

1. **Current (30K tokens)**: Already uncomfortable. ~15% of a 200K context window consumed just by loading project state. GSD uses only ~5%.

2. **50K tokens (~40 cards at mean)**: Problematic. Agents start running out of room for code context, tool outputs, and conversation. This is roughly the v5 milestone projection.

3. **100K tokens (~75 cards at mean)**: Prohibitive. Half the context window gone before any work begins. GSD would still be at ~12K.

4. **Context window limits**: Claude's 200K window means cards.json becomes truly unusable around 150K–200K tokens (60–80% of window). GSD's selective approach never hits this wall.

### The Core Problem

**GSD scales O(1) per session** — you only load what you need for the current task. Codebase files + current phase plan = constant ~10K tokens regardless of project history.

**Burrow scales O(n) per session** — every card ever written loads every time. Historical decisions, completed milestones, archived research — all of it consumes tokens on every read.

### Mitigation Strategies (if Burrow replaces GSD)

1. **Selective card loading**: A `read --subtree <id>` that only returns a branch, not the whole file. CLI already has `read` per card — the issue is the CLAUDE.md instruction to "read cards.json on session start."
2. **Card archival with cold storage**: Archived cards excluded from default load. Only pull them with `--include-archived`.
3. **Body truncation on list/dump**: Show titles + metadata but not full bodies unless requested. A `--depth` flag already exists.
4. **Multi-file sharding**: Split cards across files by milestone/topic. Breaks the single-file simplicity promise.
5. **Summary cards**: Instead of storing full research in card bodies, store summaries with references to external docs.

### Key Insight

The 71KB "v1.2 Packaging & Distribution" card reveals the real issue: **card bodies are unbounded**. A single card with detailed research can blow up the file. GSD naturally caps this by putting research in its own file that only loads when relevant.

**Recommendation**: Before replacing GSD, Burrow needs a selective loading story. The simplest path: change the session-start instruction from "read cards.json" to "run `dump --depth 1`" (titles only, ~2K tokens), then `read <id>` for specific cards as needed. This would make Burrow O(1) per session like GSD.

children.title: Selective loading strategies
children.body:
# Selective Loading Strategies — Research Findings

## Current State
- cards.json: 118 KB, ~30k tokens (157 cards: 111 active, 46 archived)
- Body content: 78k chars (~65% of file size) — the primary bloat driver
- Title content: 4k chars — tiny by comparison
- JSON structural overhead (keys, braces, indentation): ~20% of file

## Strategy 1: Index-Only Loading

**Concept**: Load full tree structure with id, title, archived, hasBody, childCount — no body content.

**CLI design**:
```
burrow index                    # nested tree, active only
burrow index --include-archived # nested tree, all cards
burrow index --flat             # flat array with parentId field
```

**Output**: JSON to stdout (not rendered — meant for agent consumption on session start).

**Token savings**:
- Nested index (active only): ~6,200 tokens (79% reduction)
- Nested index (all cards): ~9,800 tokens (67% reduction)
- Flat index (all cards): ~6,500 tokens (78% reduction)

**Implementation**: Low complexity. Add `index` command to burrow-tools.cjs. Walk tree, strip body fields, output JSON. No changes to mongoose.cjs needed — just a new CLI command with a simple recursive mapper.

**Verdict**: Best bang-for-buck. Single highest-impact change. Should be Strategy #1 to implement.

## Strategy 2: Lazy Body Fetching

**Concept**: Agent loads index on session start, then fetches individual card bodies on demand via `read <id> --full`.

**CLI design**: Already exists — `burrow read <id> --full` returns the full body. No new commands needed.

**Agent workflow**:
1. Session start: `burrow index` → agent gets full tree map (~6k tokens)
2. Agent identifies relevant cards by title/structure
3. Agent runs `burrow read <id>` for specific cards it needs
4. Each read costs ~50-200 tokens per card

**Implications**:
- Requires 2+ CLI calls instead of 1 (Read tool calls are cheap but not free)
- Agent must reason about which cards to read — adds cognitive overhead
- Works well for focused sessions (e.g., "work on feature X")
- Works poorly for broad sessions (e.g., "what's the project status?")

**Implementation**: Zero — already works today. Just needs the `index` command from Strategy 1.

**Verdict**: Natural companion to Strategy 1. The two together form a complete lazy-loading system.

## Strategy 3: Subtree Extraction

**Concept**: Extract a single card and all its descendants as a standalone JSON blob.

**CLI design**:
```
burrow subtree <id>             # full subtree as JSON
burrow subtree <id> --depth 2   # depth-limited subtree
```

**Output**: JSON blob of the card with nested children (same shape as cards.json but rooted at the specified card).

**Token cost**: Varies by subtree. Example measured subtree: 1,500 tokens for a card with ~10 descendants.

**Implementation**: Medium complexity. In mongoose.cjs, `findById` already returns the card with its full children array — that IS the subtree. The CLI command just needs to:
1. `storage.load(cwd)`
2. `tree.findById(data, id)`
3. `JSON.stringify(card)` → stdout

Could optionally strip archived cards or apply depth limits using existing `renderTree` logic.

**Verdict**: Useful for agents working on a specific branch of the tree. Complements lazy loading for "give me everything about topic X" workflows.

## Strategy 4: Depth-Limited Initial Load

**Concept**: The initial session load itself is depth-limited, not just `read` commands.

**Current state**: `read --depth N` already limits children rendering. `renderTree` supports depth parameter.

**CLI design**:
```
burrow index --depth 1    # root-level cards only (~760 tokens!)
burrow index --depth 2    # two levels deep (~1,600 tokens)
```

**Token savings at each depth** (active only):
- depth=1: 760 tokens (97% reduction!)
- depth=2: 1,626 tokens (95% reduction)
- depth=3: 5,420 tokens (82% reduction)
- depth=4: 6,098 tokens (80% reduction)

**Implementation**: Low complexity if combined with `index` command. The buildIndex function just takes a maxDepth parameter and stops recursing. Nearly identical to renderTree's depth logic.

**Verdict**: Extremely powerful when combined with Strategy 1. An agent that loads `burrow index --depth 2` on session start uses only 1,600 tokens — a 95% reduction from today's 30k.

## Strategy 5: Body-Excluded JSON Export

**Concept**: `dump --no-body` flag that outputs the full rendered tree without body content.

**CLI design**:
```
burrow dump --no-body     # full rendered tree, bodies stripped
```

**Difference from index**: `dump --no-body` produces human-readable rendered output (with box-drawing, ages, etc.). `index` produces machine-readable JSON.

**Implementation**: Low complexity. In the `dump` case in burrow-tools.cjs, pass a flag through to renderCard that suppresses the body section. Or simpler: strip body from each card before rendering.

**Verdict**: Lower priority than `index`. The rendered format is nice for humans but wasteful for agents (box-drawing chars, padding, etc. add tokens without adding information). The `index` command serves the primary use case better.

## Recommended Implementation Order

1. **`burrow index` command** (Strategy 1) — highest impact, lowest effort
   - Supports `--depth N`, `--include-archived`, `--flat` flags
   - Returns clean JSON, not rendered output
   - Estimated effort: ~50 lines in burrow-tools.cjs, ~20 lines helper in mongoose.cjs

2. **Lazy body fetching** (Strategy 2) — free, already works with `read <id>`

3. **`burrow subtree <id>`** (Strategy 3) — useful for focused work
   - Estimated effort: ~30 lines in burrow-tools.cjs

4. **`dump --no-body`** (Strategy 5) — nice-to-have
   - Estimated effort: ~10 lines (flag passthrough)

## Session Start Workflow (Post-Implementation)

**Today**: Agent reads cards.json via Read tool → 30k tokens consumed
**After**: Agent runs `burrow index --depth 2` → 1,600 tokens consumed, then `burrow read <id>` as needed

That's a 95% reduction in session start cost, with no loss of capability — the agent can still access any card body on demand.

children.title: Archived card storage: prune, export, or split?
children.body:
# Archived Card Storage Strategies — Research

## Current Implementation

Archive today is a **boolean flag** (`archived: true/false`) on each card. `archiveCard`/`unarchiveCard` in mongoose.cjs recursively set this flag on a card and all descendants. Archived cards stay in `cards.json` forever — they are simply filtered out by `renderTree` (via `shouldInclude`) and `searchCards` (which skips `card.archived`). The storage layer (warren.cjs) reads/writes a single `cards.json` with atomic write + backup.

Key facts:
- IDs are 8-char hex, referenced positionally (parent-child nesting), no cross-references by ID
- `findById` always searches the full tree (no archive filter) — unarchive works because archived cards are still findable
- `--include-archived` and `--archived-only` flags exist on `read` and `dump` commands
- Storage is single-file with atomic write (tmp+rename) and .bak backup

---

## Strategy 1: Prune to Separate Archive File

**Concept**: On archive, move the card subtree from `cards.json` to `cards.archive.json`. Unarchive reverses this.

**Trade-offs**:
- (+) Clean separation — active file stays small
- (+) Reversible — unarchive moves cards back
- (+) Archive file can be `.gitignore`d if desired
- (-) Two-file atomicity problem: if one write succeeds but the other fails, data is lost or duplicated. Need a transaction mechanism (write both to tmp, then rename both — but `rename` isn't atomic across two files)
- (-) `findById` must search both files for unarchive, `--include-archived` queries, and any future cross-referencing
- (-) Card hierarchy breaks if you archive a child but not its parent — where does it go in the archive file? Need to either (a) require archiving whole subtrees only, or (b) store orphaned cards with metadata about their original parent path
- (-) Moderate implementation complexity: storage layer needs dual-file load/save, mongoose operations need merged-tree awareness

**Implementation complexity**: Medium-high. warren.cjs needs `loadArchive`/`saveArchive`, a transactional `saveBoth()`. CLI `archive` command becomes a cross-file move. Every query with `--include-archived` needs merged tree construction.

---

## Strategy 2: Export to Markdown Snapshots

**Concept**: On archive, export the subtree as a structured markdown document (e.g., `.planning/burrow/archive/milestone-title-2026-03.md`), then delete from `cards.json`. One-way operation.

**Trade-offs**:
- (+) Maximally reduces `cards.json` size — archived content fully removed
- (+) Human-readable archive — great for git history and documentation
- (+) Simple to implement: serialize subtree to markdown, delete card, write file
- (+) No cross-file query complexity (archived content is out of Burrow's domain)
- (-) **Irreversible** — cannot unarchive. To restore, user must manually recreate cards
- (-) Loses structured data (IDs, timestamps, archive flags) unless embedded as frontmatter
- (-) Breaks the "unarchive" contract — users currently expect archived = recoverable
- (-) Multiple archive files accumulate in the directory

**Implementation complexity**: Low. Add an `exportSubtreeToMarkdown()` helper and a new CLI command (`archive --export` or `snapshot`). No changes to existing load/save.

---

## Strategy 3: Multi-File Split by Archive Status

**Concept**: Two managed files — `cards.json` (active) and `cards.archived.json` (archived). CLI transparently queries both when needed.

**Trade-offs**:
- (+) Active file stays lean by default
- (+) Reversible — unarchive moves cards back to active file
- (+) Conceptually clean — mirrors the existing `--include-archived` flag semantics
- (-) Same atomicity problem as Strategy 1 (two-file writes)
- (-) Same orphan problem: archived child of active parent creates structural issues. Must archive entire subtrees or store flattened cards with path metadata
- (-) `findById` must search both files for unarchive
- (-) Every mutation (add, edit, move) that could target archived cards needs dual-file awareness
- (-) Essentially the same as Strategy 1 with a different name — no meaningful architectural difference

**Implementation complexity**: Medium-high. Same as Strategy 1 in practice.

---

## Strategy 4: Time-Based Pruning

**Concept**: Archived cards older than N days are automatically exported (markdown or JSON) and removed from `cards.json`. Could run on `load()` or as a separate `prune` command.

**Trade-offs**:
- (+) Automatic — no manual intervention needed
- (+) Graduated: recently archived cards remain recoverable (unarchive works), old ones get exported
- (+) Can combine with Strategy 2 (export to markdown on prune)
- (-) Surprising data loss if N is too small or user forgets about the pruning
- (-) Needs a timestamp for "when was this archived?" — currently only `created` exists, no `archivedAt` field
- (-) Auto-pruning on `load()` means a read operation has side effects (bad practice). Separate command is safer but requires discipline
- (-) Requires adding `archivedAt` field to card schema — migration needed

**Implementation complexity**: Medium. Needs schema change (`archivedAt` timestamp), a `prune` command, and an export mechanism. The pruning logic itself is simple (walk tree, check dates, export + delete old archived subtrees).

---

## Strategy 5: Compression (Skeleton Mode)

**Concept**: Keep archived cards in `cards.json` but strip bodies, retaining only titles and structure. Full content could optionally be exported before stripping.

**Trade-offs**:
- (+) Single-file simplicity preserved — no multi-file complexity
- (+) Archived cards remain findable, searchable by title, and visible in `--include-archived` views
- (+) Unarchive still works (restores visibility), though body content is gone
- (+) Significant size reduction — bodies are the bulk of card data (research notes, plans, etc.)
- (+) Zero changes to storage layer, tree operations, or CLI flags
- (-) **Lossy** — body content is permanently destroyed (unless exported first)
- (-) Partial information loss is arguably worse than full removal (Strategy 2) because users might expect bodies to still be there
- (-) Could combine with pre-strip export, but then it's just Strategy 2 with extra steps

**Implementation complexity**: Very low. Modify `archiveCard` to optionally clear bodies. No storage or CLI changes needed.

---

## Comparison Matrix

| Strategy | File count | Reversible | Complexity | Size reduction | Data safety |
|----------|-----------|------------|------------|----------------|-------------|
| 1. Separate archive file | 2 | Yes | Medium-high | High | Medium (atomicity risk) |
| 2. Markdown export | 1 + N | No | Low | Maximum | Low (irreversible) |
| 3. Multi-file split | 2 | Yes | Medium-high | High | Medium (atomicity risk) |
| 4. Time-based pruning | 1 + N | Partial | Medium | Graduated | Medium (auto-deletion) |
| 5. Compression/skeleton | 1 | Partial | Very low | Medium | Medium (lossy) |

---

## Recommendation

**Start with Strategy 5 (compression) as the v1 solution, with Strategy 2 (markdown export) as an opt-in complement.**

Rationale:
1. **Single-file is Burrow's strength.** Strategies 1 and 3 introduce multi-file atomicity problems that fundamentally undermine the simplicity that makes Burrow valuable. The engineering cost of transactional two-file writes with proper rollback is disproportionate to the benefit.

2. **Compression is nearly free.** Adding a `--strip-bodies` flag to `archive` (or making it default after a grace period) requires ~5 lines of code change in `archiveCard`. Bodies are where the bloat lives — a milestone with 20 research cards might have 50KB of body text but only 2KB of structure.

3. **Markdown export is the escape hatch.** For users who want full history, a `snapshot` command that exports a subtree to markdown before archiving gives them a human-readable record. This is a one-way operation by design — it's documentation, not a backup.

4. **Avoid time-based anything.** Automatic data deletion is surprising and dangerous. If pruning is needed, make it explicit (`burrow prune --older-than 90d`).

5. **Defer multi-file to v2+.** If single-file truly becomes a bottleneck (thousands of active cards), multi-file support is a v2 concern. By then, Burrow will have more infrastructure to handle it properly.

### Suggested implementation order:
1. Add `archivedAt` timestamp to archive operation (future-proofing, ~trivial)
2. Add `snapshot` command: export subtree to markdown, optionally delete after
3. Add `--strip` flag to `archive`: clear bodies on archive (keeps skeleton)
4. Later: explicit `prune` command combining snapshot + delete for old archived cards

children.title: GSD agent compatibility path
children.body:
# GSD Agent Compatibility Path — Research Findings

## Overview

GSD is a multi-agent planning/execution system with 12 specialized agents (planner, executor, verifier, researcher, roadmapper, debugger, etc.) and 32 slash commands. These agents currently depend heavily on a file-based directory structure under `.planning/`, with hardcoded paths woven through both agent prompts and a supporting CLI tool (`gsd-tools.cjs`).

### Coupling Surface Area

- **166 references** to specific `.md` filenames (STATE.md, ROADMAP.md, PLAN.md, etc.) across 9 agent definitions
- **44 references** to `.planning/` paths across 12 GSD slash commands
- **gsd-tools.cjs** (core.cjs, init.cjs, state.cjs, roadmap.cjs, phase.cjs, verify.cjs) — the entire tooling layer assumes `.planning/` filesystem layout with nested phase directories
- Agents use `gsd-tools.cjs init` commands that return JSON with `phase_dir`, `state_path`, `roadmap_path`, etc. — these are actual filesystem paths
- Agents directly `cat`, `grep`, `ls` against `.planning/` paths in their prompts

### Key GSD Artifacts

| Artifact | Location | Used By |
|----------|----------|---------|
| STATE.md | .planning/STATE.md | executor, planner, roadmapper, resume |
| ROADMAP.md | .planning/ROADMAP.md | planner, verifier, roadmapper, executor |
| REQUIREMENTS.md | .planning/REQUIREMENTS.md | planner, verifier, roadmapper |
| config.json | .planning/config.json | all agents (via gsd-tools) |
| PLAN.md | .planning/phases/XX-name/ | planner, executor, verifier |
| SUMMARY.md | .planning/phases/XX-name/ | executor, verifier, integration-checker |
| RESEARCH.md | .planning/phases/XX-name/ | researcher, planner |
| VERIFICATION.md | .planning/phases/XX-name/ | verifier |
| CONTEXT.md | .planning/phases/XX-name/ | planner, researcher |
| research/* | .planning/research/ | project-researcher, synthesizer, roadmapper |
| codebase/* | .planning/codebase/ | codebase-mapper |
| debug/* | .planning/debug/ | debugger |

---

## Approach 1: Teach GSD Agents to Read/Write Burrow Directly

### How it would work
Replace all file reads/writes in agent prompts with Burrow CLI calls. Instead of `cat .planning/STATE.md`, agents would run `burrow read <state-card-id>`. Instead of `Write .planning/phases/XX/XX-01-PLAN.md`, they'd run `burrow add --parent <phase-card> --title "Plan 01" --body "..."`.

### What changes
- **All 12 agent prompts** need rewriting — every file path reference, every `cat`/`grep`/`ls` command, every `Write` tool instruction
- **gsd-tools.cjs** would need a complete rewrite — init commands, state management, roadmap parsing all assume files
- Agents need a way to discover card IDs. Currently Burrow uses 8-char hex IDs (e.g., `dc4ad1c7`). Agents would need to either: (a) be given IDs in their spawn context, (b) use `burrow find` to search by title, or (c) navigate by convention
- **Markdown content model mismatch**: GSD artifacts are rich markdown documents (1-5KB each) with YAML frontmatter, tables, nested headers. Burrow card bodies are free-form text but the model is title+body, not full documents. A PLAN.md with 200 lines of structured content doesn't map cleanly to a single card body

### Feasibility: LOW
### Effort: VERY HIGH (weeks of work)
### Risk: HIGH — fundamentally different data models

**Verdict**: The data model mismatch is the killer. GSD artifacts are structured documents with parsed frontmatter (YAML), sections that downstream tools grep through, and content that gets committed to git as human-readable files. Stuffing all of that into card bodies loses the structure that makes GSD work. You'd essentially be reimplementing a filesystem inside Burrow.

---

## Approach 2: Burrow as Sync Layer

### How it would work
Burrow holds the source of truth for project state. On-demand, it generates the `.planning/` directory structure that GSD agents expect. After GSD agents modify files, changes sync back to Burrow cards.

### Complexity analysis
- **Burrow -> Files (export)**: Need to map card tree to directory structure. A "Phase 1" card with children "Plan 01", "Plan 02" becomes `.planning/phases/01-name/01-01-PLAN.md`, `.planning/phases/01-name/01-02-PLAN.md`. STATE.md, ROADMAP.md would be generated from designated cards. This requires a rigid mapping convention.
- **Files -> Burrow (import)**: After an executor writes a SUMMARY.md, detect the change and update the corresponding Burrow card. This is the hard part — you need file watching or manual sync triggers, diff detection, and conflict resolution.
- **Bidirectional sync is inherently complex**: What happens when both sides change? When an agent creates a new file that has no card? When a card is archived but the file still exists?
- **Frontmatter/metadata**: GSD tools parse YAML frontmatter from PLAN.md files. This metadata would need to live somewhere in the card structure.

### Feasibility: MEDIUM
### Effort: HIGH (1-2 weeks)
### Risk: MEDIUM — sync bugs, state drift (ironic given Burrow's purpose)

**Verdict**: This approach has the ironic problem of introducing exactly the kind of state drift that Burrow was built to prevent. Two sources of truth that must stay in sync is an anti-pattern Burrow was designed to eliminate.

---

## Approach 3: Adapter Pattern (Export Views)

### How it would work
Burrow CLI gets an `export` command: `burrow export --as-gsd` generates the full `.planning/` directory structure. GSD agents work completely unmodified. After GSD work is done, `burrow import --from-gsd` reads the files back into cards.

### Key difference from Approach 2
This is one-directional at a time. No live sync. Explicit export before GSD runs, explicit import after. The user (or a wrapper script) orchestrates the handoff.

### What's needed
1. **Export mapping**: Convention that maps card hierarchy to GSD file layout
   - Root card "Project" -> .planning/PROJECT.md (body = file content)
   - Root card "Roadmap" -> .planning/ROADMAP.md
   - Root card "State" -> .planning/STATE.md
   - "Phase 1: Name" card with children -> .planning/phases/01-name/ with child cards becoming PLAN.md, RESEARCH.md, etc.
2. **Import mapping**: Reverse of export — scan .planning/, find files, update or create corresponding cards
3. **Conflict strategy**: Export overwrites files. Import overwrites cards. Simple, predictable.

### Feasibility: HIGH
### Effort: MEDIUM (3-5 days for a working prototype)
### Risk: LOW — GSD agents are untouched, Burrow is untouched, adapter is isolated

**Verdict**: Most practical short-term path. Burrow and GSD stay decoupled. The adapter is a thin translation layer. But it's still two representations of the same data, just with explicit sync points instead of live sync. Good enough for a v1 integration.

---

## Approach 4: Convention-Based Discovery

### How it would work
Establish naming conventions for Burrow cards that let GSD agents find what they need. A phase card always has children named "Research", "Plan 01", "Plan 02", etc. Agents navigate by querying: `burrow find "State"` to find the STATE card, `burrow find "Phase 3"` to find phase 3.

### Required conventions
- Top-level cards: "Project", "Roadmap", "State", "Requirements", "Config"
- Phase cards: "Phase 01: Name" with children "Research", "Plan 01", "Plan 02", "Summary 01", "Verification"
- Research cards: "Research" container with children "Stack", "Features", "Architecture", "Pitfalls", "Summary"

### Problems
1. **Name collisions**: `burrow find "Research"` could match multiple cards across phases. Need path-aware search or parent scoping.
2. **Agent prompt changes**: Still need to rewrite all 12 agent prompts to use Burrow CLI instead of file ops. Same effort as Approach 1.
3. **Content model mismatch**: Same problem as Approach 1 — rich markdown documents don't map cleanly to card bodies.
4. **gsd-tools.cjs**: The entire tooling layer still needs rewriting.

### Feasibility: LOW-MEDIUM
### Effort: HIGH (same as Approach 1, but with conventions instead of hardcoded IDs)
### Risk: MEDIUM — conventions are fragile, naming drift possible

**Verdict**: This is really Approach 1 with a discovery mechanism bolted on. It doesn't solve the fundamental data model mismatch. However, the convention idea is valuable as a building block for other approaches — it defines the card structure that export/import (Approach 3) would use.

---

## Recommendation

### Best path: Approach 3 (Adapter Pattern) with Approach 4 conventions

**Phase 1 — Define card conventions (Approach 4 contribution)**
Establish the card tree structure that represents a GSD project:
```
Project (body = PROJECT.md content)
├── Roadmap (body = ROADMAP.md content)
├── State (body = STATE.md content)
├── Requirements (body = REQUIREMENTS.md content)
├── Config (body = config.json content)
├── Research
│   ├── Stack (body = STACK.md)
│   ├── Features (body = FEATURES.md)
│   └── ...
└── Phases
    ├── Phase 01: Name
    │   ├── Research (body = 01-RESEARCH.md)
    │   ├── Plan 01 (body = 01-01-PLAN.md)
    │   ├── Summary 01 (body = 01-01-SUMMARY.md)
    │   └── Verification (body = 01-VERIFICATION.md)
    └── Phase 02: Name
        └── ...
```

**Phase 2 — Build export/import adapter (Approach 3)**
- `burrow export --as-gsd` — walks the convention tree, writes `.planning/` files
- `burrow import --from-gsd` — scans `.planning/`, updates/creates cards
- Both are idempotent and explicit (no magic sync)

**Phase 3 — Optional: GSD-native Burrow mode (Approach 1, long-term)**
If the adapter proves valuable, consider a deeper integration where gsd-tools.cjs can optionally use Burrow as its backend. This would be a `storage_backend: "burrow"` config option where gsd-tools reads/writes cards instead of files. The agent prompts would stay unchanged because gsd-tools abstracts the storage — agents interact with gsd-tools, not with files directly.

### Why this ordering
1. Conventions are cheap (just documentation) and prerequisite for everything else
2. The adapter gives immediate value with zero GSD changes
3. Native integration is the most work but gsd-tools.cjs already provides a natural abstraction boundary — agents talk to gsd-tools, not to files directly. This is the seam where Burrow could slot in.

### Key insight about gsd-tools.cjs
The most promising integration point is **gsd-tools.cjs**, not the agent prompts. Agents already don't touch files directly for many operations — they call `gsd-tools.cjs state advance-plan`, `gsd-tools.cjs roadmap update-plan-progress`, etc. If gsd-tools.cjs could read/write Burrow cards instead of markdown files, agents would mostly work unchanged. The remaining friction is where agents use `cat`, `grep`, `Write` directly on files — but these could be progressively replaced with gsd-tools commands.

### Effort estimates
| Phase | Effort | Dependencies |
|-------|--------|-------------|
| Card conventions | 1 day | None |
| Export adapter | 2-3 days | Conventions |
| Import adapter | 2-3 days | Conventions |
| gsd-tools Burrow backend | 1-2 weeks | Conventions, deep GSD knowledge |

children.title: Migration path for existing .planning/ projects
children.body:
## Migration Path: .planning/ to Burrow

### 1. Mapping: GSD Artifacts to Burrow Cards

#### Structural Mapping

| GSD Artifact | Burrow Card | Card Body Contains |
|---|---|---|
| PROJECT.md | Root "Project" card | Core value, requirements, branding, decisions |
| ROADMAP.md | "Roadmap" card under Project | Phase list, progress table |
| STATE.md | "State" card under Project | Current position, metrics, continuity |
| MILESTONES.md | "Milestones" card under Project | Per-milestone stats and accomplishments |
| RETROSPECTIVE.md | "Retrospective" card under Project | Lessons learned per milestone |
| milestones/v1.0-ROADMAP.md | Child of Milestones card: "v1.0 MVP" | Phase list, key decisions |
| milestones/v1.0-REQUIREMENTS.md | Child of v1.0 card: "Requirements" | Requirement checklist |
| milestones/v1.0-phases/01-core-engine/ | Child of v1.0 card: "Phase 1: Core Engine" | Phase overview |
| 01-RESEARCH.md | Child of Phase 1 card: "Research" | Full research markdown |
| 01-01-PLAN.md | Child of Phase 1 card: "Plan 01" | Full plan markdown |
| 01-01-SUMMARY.md | Child of Phase 1 card: "Summary 01" | Execution summary |
| 01-VERIFICATION.md | Child of Phase 1 card: "Verification" | Test results |
| 01-VALIDATION.md | Child of Phase 1 card: "Validation" | Requirement validation |
| 01-CONTEXT.md | Child of Phase 1 card: "Context" | Decisions and constraints |
| config.json | Not migrated — remains as file or becomes a settings card |
| codebase/*.md | "Codebase" parent card with children per file |
| research/*.md | "Research" parent card with children per file |
| debug/*.md | "Debug" parent card with children per file |
| quick/N-*/ | "Quick fixes" parent card, one child per quick fix |

#### Tree Structure

```
Project
├── Roadmap
├── State
├── Milestones
│   ├── v1.0 MVP
│   │   ├── Requirements
│   │   ├── Phase 1: Core Engine
│   │   │   ├── Research
│   │   │   ├── Context
│   │   │   ├── Plan 01 + Summary 01
│   │   │   ├── Plan 02 + Summary 02
│   │   │   ├── Verification
│   │   │   └── Validation
│   │   ├── Phase 2: Schema, Views, Archive
│   │   │   └── ... (same pattern)
│   │   └── Milestone Audit
│   └── v1.1 Rendering & Ergonomics
│       └── ... (same pattern)
├── Retrospective
├── Codebase
│   ├── Architecture
│   ├── Structure
│   └── ...
├── Research
│   ├── Storage
│   ├── Architecture
│   └── ...
└── Debug
    ├── move-cmd-broken
    └── ...
```

#### Markdown Preservation

Card bodies accept arbitrary strings — full markdown is preserved as-is. No size limit enforced. The 01-RESEARCH.md file (551 lines, ~27KB) would become a single card body. This works but has token cost implications (see sibling card 7a5f1dd1).

**Recommendation**: For completed/historical artifacts, store a **summary** in the card body and note the original file path. For active artifacts, store the full content. The import command should support `--summarize` (title + first paragraph only) and `--full` (entire file) modes.

### 2. The Import Command: `burrow import`

#### Proposed CLI

```
burrow import <path> [--parent <id>] [--mode full|summary|title-only] [--dry-run] [--recursive]
```

**Single file import:**
```bash
burrow import .planning/PROJECT.md --parent <project-card-id> --mode full
```
- Title: derived from first `# Heading` in the file, or filename
- Body: entire file content (full mode) or first section (summary mode)

**Directory import:**
```bash
burrow import .planning/milestones/v1.0-phases/01-core-engine/ --parent <v1.0-card-id> --recursive
```
- Creates a parent card per directory
- Creates child cards per file within
- Preserves directory nesting as card nesting

**Full GSD import:**
```bash
burrow import .planning/ --mode summary --dry-run
```
- Dry run shows what would be created without mutating
- Uses the structural mapping above

#### Implementation Notes

- **Title extraction**: Parse first `# heading` line. Fall back to filename without extension.
- **Parent inference**: Top-level .planning/ files → root children. Milestone dirs → under a "Milestones" parent. Phase dirs → under their milestone.
- **Idempotency**: Check for existing card with same title under same parent before creating. Skip or update.
- **Output**: Print created card IDs so user can verify structure with `dump`.

### 3. Strategy A: Coexistence Period

#### How It Works

1. Import completed milestones into Burrow (v1.0, v1.1 → archived cards)
2. Keep .planning/ files for any in-progress milestone
3. New milestones tracked exclusively in Burrow cards
4. GSD agents continue reading .planning/ for legacy phases
5. Cutover when all in-progress .planning/ work completes

#### Cutover Trigger

When STATE.md shows `status: between_milestones` and no active phase exists in .planning/, migration is safe.

#### Risks

- **Dual-state drift**: Two sources of truth. If someone updates a .planning/ file AND a Burrow card about the same topic, they diverge silently.
- **Agent confusion**: CLAUDE.md says "read cards.json on session start" but GSD agents also read STATE.md and ROADMAP.md. The agent loads both and may get conflicting signals.
- **Increased token cost**: Loading both systems costs more than either alone. During coexistence you pay the sum: cards.json (~30K tokens) + GSD core files (~6K tokens) = ~36K tokens.
- **No natural forcing function**: Without pressure to finish the migration, coexistence can become permanent. Set a deadline (e.g., "delete .planning/ markdown after next milestone ships").

#### Mitigation

- Designate .planning/ as READ-ONLY during coexistence. All new writes go to Burrow.
- Add a CLAUDE.md note: "Do not modify .planning/*.md files. Use Burrow cards for all new state."
- Set a calendar date for full cutover.

### 4. Strategy B: Big Bang Cutover

#### When Appropriate

- Project is between milestones (current state — yes, Burrow qualifies right now)
- No in-progress work that agents are actively reading from .planning/ files
- Team is comfortable with the new system (i.e., has used Burrow cards for at least one milestone)

#### Steps

1. `git checkout -b migrate-to-burrow` — work on a branch
2. `burrow import .planning/ --mode summary --dry-run` — preview the migration
3. `burrow import .planning/ --mode summary` — execute
4. `burrow dump --depth 2` — verify tree structure looks right
5. Manually review key cards: `burrow read <project-card-id> --full`
6. Update CLAUDE.md: remove GSD references, keep Burrow instructions
7. `git rm -r .planning/*.md .planning/milestones/ .planning/phases/ .planning/research/ .planning/codebase/ .planning/debug/ .planning/quick/`
8. Keep `.planning/burrow/` and `.planning/config.json`
9. Commit and verify the agent can operate with Burrow-only

#### Risk

- If the import misses something, the information is gone (from active state — still in git history)
- Agent workflows that hardcode .planning/ file paths break immediately
- No gradual learning curve — agent must use Burrow for everything from day one

#### Mitigation

- Git history preserves everything. `git show HEAD~1:.planning/PROJECT.md` recovers any file.
- Run `burrow export` (see rollback strategy below) immediately after import to verify roundtrip.

### 5. Strategy C (Recommended): Archival Import + Forward-Only

**This is the recommended approach for projects like Burrow itself.**

#### Rationale

Completed milestones (v1.0, v1.1) are historical artifacts. They are rarely read, never modified. Importing them in full into cards wastes tokens. Instead:

1. **Don't import historical .planning/ content into Burrow at all.** Leave it in git history or a `.planning/archive/` directory.
2. **Create lightweight reference cards** pointing to the archived files:
   ```
   burrow add --title "v1.0 MVP (archived)" --body "Shipped 2026-03-09. 5 phases, 10 plans. Archives: milestones/v1.0-ROADMAP.md" --parent <milestones-id>
   ```
3. **Use Burrow natively for all future work.** New milestones, new phases, new research — all as cards from the start.
4. **Delete or archive .planning/ markdown files** (except config.json and burrow/).

#### Why This Beats Full Import

- No token cost for historical data that is never read
- No risk of import bugs corrupting active state
- Clean separation: past = .planning/ archive, future = Burrow cards
- Simplest possible migration — takes 10 minutes, not an hour

### 6. User Experience Walkthrough

#### Recommended Migration (Strategy C)

```bash
# Step 1: Verify you're between milestones
cat .planning/STATE.md | head -10
# Confirm: status: between_milestones

# Step 2: Create project structure in Burrow
burrow add --title "Project" --body "$(head -20 .planning/PROJECT.md)"
# Returns: id abc123

burrow add --title "Milestones" --parent abc123
# Returns: id def456

burrow add --title "v1.0 MVP (shipped 2026-03-09)" --parent def456 \
  --body "5 phases, 10 plans, 150 tests. See git history for full .planning/ archives."

burrow add --title "v1.1 Rendering & Ergonomics (shipped 2026-03-14)" --parent def456 \
  --body "3 phases, 8 plans, 240 tests. See git history for full .planning/ archives."

# Step 3: Move active backlog items (already in Burrow)
# The Backlog card (d6e53b29) already exists — no migration needed

# Step 4: Archive .planning/ markdown
mkdir -p .planning/archive
mv .planning/PROJECT.md .planning/ROADMAP.md .planning/STATE.md \
   .planning/MILESTONES.md .planning/RETROSPECTIVE.md .planning/archive/
mv .planning/milestones .planning/research .planning/codebase \
   .planning/debug .planning/quick .planning/phases .planning/archive/

# Step 5: Update CLAUDE.md — remove GSD file references
# Step 6: Commit
git add -A && git commit -m "chore: migrate planning state to Burrow cards"
```

Total time: ~10 minutes. Zero risk of data loss (git history + archive dir).

### 7. Rollback Strategy: `burrow export`

If Burrow-as-planning doesn't work, the user needs to get back to markdown.

#### Proposed Command

```
burrow export [<id>] [--format md|json] [--output <dir>]
```

**Export all cards as markdown files:**
```bash
burrow export --format md --output .planning/restored/
```

- Each card becomes a markdown file: `<title-slugified>.md`
- Card children become files in a subdirectory: `<title-slugified>/`
- Card body becomes file content with `# Title` header prepended
- Archived cards go into an `archived/` subdirectory

**Export a subtree:**
```bash
burrow export abc123 --format md --output .planning/milestones/
```

#### Implementation Priority

The export command should ship **before or alongside** the import command. Users will not adopt a system they cannot leave. This is table stakes for trust.

### 8. Summary of Recommendations

1. **Don't build a full automated import yet.** The mapping is complex (105 files, mixed structures) and completed milestones don't need to be in Burrow.
2. **Build `burrow export` first.** This is the safety net that makes adoption reversible.
3. **Use Strategy C (archival import + forward-only)** for existing projects. Archive .planning/ files, create lightweight reference cards, use Burrow natively for new work.
4. **Build `burrow import` later** as a convenience for new GSD users who want to switch mid-milestone. Single-file import is the MVP; directory-recursive import is a stretch goal.
5. **Address selective loading first** (see sibling card 7a5f1dd1). Without it, Burrow-as-planning hits token cost walls that make the whole migration pointless.
6. **Set a coexistence deadline.** If using Strategy A, commit to a cutover date. Open-ended dual-state always drifts.

children.title: Multi-file split design for planning scale
children.body:
# Multi-File Split Strategy Research

## Current Architecture Summary

The current system is clean and simple:
- **Storage** (`warren.cjs`): `load(cwd)` reads a single `cards.json`, `save(cwd, data)` writes it atomically with backup.
- **Tree engine** (`mongoose.cjs`): All operations (`findById`, `findParent`, `getPath`, `moveCard`, etc.) take a single `data` object with `data.cards` array and do recursive walks.
- **CLI** (`burrow-tools.cjs`): Every command does `load → operate → save` on that one file.

**Key observation**: The tree engine is already file-agnostic — it operates on a `data` object, not on files. The coupling to a single file lives entirely in `warren.cjs`. This is a good separation point.

---

## Strategy 1: Split by Milestone

Each milestone (e.g., v1.0, v1.1, v1.2) gets its own JSON file. Root `cards.json` contains top-level structure with stub references.

### How it works
- `cards.json` contains top-level cards with a `$ref: "cards-v1.2.json"` pointer instead of `children`.
- When the tree engine encounters a `$ref`, the storage layer loads that file and splices in the subtree.

### Analysis
| Aspect | Assessment |
|--------|------------|
| **ID lookup** | Must scan all referenced files. Requires a registry/index or brute-force multi-file search. |
| **find/path/dump** | `dump` must load all files. `path` must resolve across file boundaries. `find` must search all files. |
| **move across files** | Complex: remove from source file's tree, add to target file's tree, save both files atomically. |
| **Implementation** | Medium-high. Requires `$ref` resolution in storage layer, multi-file atomic saves, and an ID-to-file index for O(1) lookup. |

### Verdict
Tight coupling to project naming conventions. Forces the user to organize by milestone, which may not match their mental model. The `$ref` mechanism adds indirection that complicates every operation.

---

## Strategy 2: Split by Depth

Top N levels live in `cards.json`. Deeper subtrees are automatically extracted to separate files (e.g., `subtree-{id}.json`).

### How it works
- On `save()`, any subtree deeper than N levels is extracted: the parent card gets `children: "$file:subtree-abc123.json"`, and the subtree is written to that file.
- On `load()`, the storage layer reassembles the full tree by loading all subtree files.

### Analysis
| Aspect | Assessment |
|--------|------------|
| **ID lookup** | Same problem as Strategy 1: must reassemble full tree or maintain an index. |
| **find/path/dump** | All must load the full reassembled tree. No savings for read-heavy operations. |
| **move across files** | Auto-handled if the engine operates on the reassembled tree and re-splits on save. But the split boundary shifts on every structural change. |
| **Implementation** | Medium. Transparent to the tree engine if load/save handle assembly/disassembly. But depth is a bad heuristic — a card at depth 3 might be tiny, while depth 1 might be huge. |

### Verdict
Content-agnostic is a strength but also a weakness. Depth doesn't correlate with file size. A single deep-but-narrow branch won't benefit, while a wide shallow tree never splits. Also, repeated assemble/disassemble on every operation is wasteful.

---

## Strategy 3: Split by Archive Status

Active tree stays in `cards.json`. Archived subtrees are moved to `cards-archive.json` (or `archive/{id}.json`).

### How it works
- `archive` command moves the card subtree from `cards.json` into `cards-archive.json`.
- `unarchive` moves it back.
- Active operations (add, edit, move, find, read, dump) only touch `cards.json`.
- `--include-archived` and `--archived-only` flags load the archive file too.

### Analysis
| Aspect | Assessment |
|--------|------------|
| **ID lookup** | Active ops: only `cards.json`. Archive-aware ops: check both files. Clear, predictable. |
| **find/path/dump** | Default: only active file. With archive flags: load both. Simple branching logic. |
| **move across files** | Only happens on archive/unarchive — already an explicit user action with clear semantics. |
| **Implementation** | **Low.** `archive` already sets a flag recursively. Just add file-move logic. Storage layer needs `loadArchive()`/`saveArchive()` but tree engine is untouched. |

### Verdict
Strong candidate. Aligns with existing behavior (archived cards are already filtered out by default). The file boundary matches a semantic boundary the user already understands. However, it only helps if users actually archive old work — if they don't, the file grows forever.

---

## Strategy 4: Split by Explicit User Action

User runs `burrow split <id>` to extract a subtree into its own file. `burrow merge <id>` brings it back.

### How it works
- `split <id>` removes the subtree from `cards.json`, writes it to `subtree-{id}.json`, and leaves a stub: `{id, title, $ref: "subtree-{id}.json", children: []}`.
- `merge <id>` reverses the process.
- The storage layer resolves `$ref` on load, reassembling the full tree.

### Analysis
| Aspect | Assessment |
|--------|------------|
| **ID lookup** | If load reassembles: same as single file. If lazy: must check stubs first. |
| **find/path/dump** | Work on reassembled tree, transparent to engine. |
| **move across files** | If reassembled: transparent. Moving a card out of a split subtree just means the split file shrinks on save. |
| **Implementation** | Medium. The split/merge commands are simple. The `$ref` resolution in load/save is the real work. |

### Verdict
Maximum user control, but requires users to know when and what to split. AI agents won't naturally do this unless prompted. Good as a power-user escape hatch, not as the primary strategy.

---

## Strategy 5: Hybrid Archive-Split + Lazy Load

Combine archive-based file separation with lazy body loading for active cards.

### How it works
- **Archive split** (Strategy 3): Archived subtrees move to separate files.
- **Lazy body loading**: Card bodies over a threshold (e.g., 500 chars) are stored in `bodies/{id}.txt`. The card in `cards.json` has `body: "$body:{id}"`. Full body is loaded on-demand by `read` and `dump --full`.
- `find`, `read` (tree view), `path` never need to load body files.

### Analysis
| Aspect | Assessment |
|--------|------------|
| **ID lookup** | Same as Strategy 3 — archive split only. Body files don't affect ID resolution. |
| **find/path/dump** | `find` and `path` work on the skeletal tree (fast). `dump --full` loads all body files. `read <id>` loads one body file. |
| **move across files** | Archive boundary only. Body files are ID-keyed, so they follow their card automatically. |
| **Implementation** | Medium. Archive split is simple (Strategy 3). Lazy body requires changes to `addCard`/`editCard` to externalize bodies, and `read`/`dump` to load them. |

### Verdict
Most comprehensive but also most complex. The lazy body loading is a premature optimization — bodies are typically short (the current PREVIEW_TRUNCATE_LENGTH is 80 chars). This solves a problem that doesn't exist yet.

---

## Recommendation: Strategy 3 (Archive Split), with Strategy 4 as a Future Escape Hatch

### Why Archive Split wins

1. **Lowest implementation cost.** The archive/unarchive commands already exist. The file boundary aligns with an existing semantic boundary. Warren.cjs needs ~30 lines of new code (loadArchive, saveArchive, moveToArchive, moveFromArchive).

2. **Zero changes to mongoose.cjs.** The tree engine continues to operate on a single `data` object. The storage layer handles which file to read/write. This preserves the clean separation.

3. **Predictable UX.** Users already understand archive = "done, hide it." Making archive = "done, hide it, and shrink the active file" is a natural extension. No new commands, no new mental model.

4. **Covers the real growth scenario.** When Burrow replaces GSD, each completed milestone gets archived. That's exactly when the subtree should leave `cards.json`. The growth pattern is: work → complete → archive. Archive-split intercepts at the right moment.

5. **Active file stays small.** At any point, only in-progress work is in `cards.json`. Historical context is one `--include-archived` flag away.

### Implementation sketch

```
warren.cjs changes:
- ARCHIVE_FILE = 'cards-archive.json'
- loadArchive(cwd) → reads archive file (or returns {version:2, cards:[]})
- saveArchive(cwd, data) → atomic write like save()
- archiveCard now:
  1. Removes subtree from active data
  2. Appends to archive data (preserving original parent path as metadata)
  3. Saves both files
- unarchiveCard now:
  1. Finds card in archive data
  2. Removes from archive, adds back to active data at original location (or root)
  3. Saves both files

Commands affected:
- archive: load both → move card active→archive → save both
- unarchive: load both → move card archive→active → save both  
- read --include-archived: load both, merge for display
- dump --include-archived: load both, merge for display
- find: search active only (or both with flag)
- All other commands: unchanged, active file only
```

### When to add Strategy 4 (explicit split)

If a user has a single massive active subtree that can't be archived (e.g., an ongoing long-running initiative), Strategy 4's `split`/`merge` commands become the escape hatch. This should be a v3+ feature, built on the same `$ref` mechanism but triggered manually. The archive split handles 90% of cases; explicit split handles the remaining 10%.

### What NOT to do

- **Don't build an ID index.** With archive split, the active file is always small enough for brute-force search. An index adds complexity for no measurable gain.
- **Don't lazy-load bodies.** Card bodies are short. The cost of external body files (extra I/O, broken atomicity, orphaned files) outweighs the savings.
- **Don't split by depth or milestone.** These are solutions looking for a problem. The real problem is "completed work bloats the active file," and archive-split solves that directly.

children.title: Scalability: lazy discovery via map command
children.body:
## Insight

Burrow already has the primitives for scalable loading. The problem is the workflow instruction that tells the agent to read cards.json directly on every session start (~30k tokens and growing).

## Solution

Change the workflow so the agent never reads cards.json directly:

1. **Session start**: `burrow read` (root, depth 1 — just top-level card titles)
2. **Drill down**: `burrow read <id>` when deeper context is needed
3. **Full JSON read**: only if a mutation requires it (and the CLI handles that internally)

No new commands. No index command. The existing `read` with `--depth` already supports incremental discovery.

## Trade-off

Lose the ability to answer tree-wide questions (counting, searching across all cards) without multiple reads. Acceptable — those are rare compared to the per-session cost of full loading.

## What Changes

One line in `.claude/burrow/workflows/burrow.md` — replace 'read cards.json with the Read tool' with 'run `burrow read` via CLI'.

children.title: Performance benchmarks vs alternatives
children.body: Build a benchmark suite to measure burrow's performance against alternative agent storage solutions (flat markdown files, SQLite, etc.). Compare on read/write speed, token cost, state drift frequency, and developer ergonomics. Provides concrete data for the value proposition.

children.title: Burrow cost profiling
children.body:
# Burrow Token & Performance Cost Analysis

**Measured**: 2026-03-14 | **Dataset**: 164 cards (118 active, 46 archived) | **File**: 180,013 bytes

---

## 1. Token Cost per CLI Operation

| Command | Output (chars) | Est. Tokens | Exec Time |
|---|---|---|---|
| `read` (root, depth 1) | 2,283 | ~571 | 57ms |
| `read <id>` (card w/ 7 children) | 1,400 | ~350 | 56ms |
| `read <id> --depth 2` | 1,922 | ~480 | 67ms |
| `read <id> --full` | 1,400 | ~350 | 54ms |
| `find <query>` (1 match) | 34 | ~8 | 51ms |
| `dump` (full tree) | 11,143 | ~2,786 | 49ms |
| `path <id>` | 29 | ~7 | 57ms |
| `add --title --parent` | 777 | ~194 | 88ms |
| `remove <id>` | 41 | ~10 | 82ms |
| `edit <id> --body` | 3,353 | ~838 | 83ms |

**Key finding**: All commands complete in 49-88ms. Reads are ~50-67ms, writes ~80-88ms (file I/O).

## 2. File Size Breakdown

| Component | Bytes | % of File |
|---|---|---|
| Active content (titles + bodies) | 127,015 | 70.6% |
| Archived content (titles + bodies) | 8,383 | 4.7% |
| JSON structure (keys, braces, quotes, whitespace) | 39,183 | 21.8% |
| Metadata (IDs, dates, flags) | 5,432 | 3.0% |
| **Total** | **180,013** | **100%** |

## 3. Overhead Ratio

- **Content**: 75.3% of file is user content (titles + bodies)
- **Structural overhead**: 24.7% (JSON syntax + metadata)
- **Archived dead weight**: 4.7% of file is archived content not actively used
- In token terms: ~11,154 tokens of overhead per 33,850 tokens of content

## 4. Session-Start Loading Costs

| Method | Chars | Tokens | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| `Read` tool on cards.json | 180,013 | ~45,003 | Full file into context |
| `dump` command | 11,143 | ~2,786 | 93.8% smaller than raw file |
| `read` (root only) | 2,283 | ~571 | Just top-level cards |

**Critical insight**: `dump` is 16x cheaper than reading raw cards.json. At current size, reading the raw file costs ~45K tokens; dump costs ~2.8K tokens.

## 5. Per-Card Averages

- Avg file bytes per card: **1,098 bytes** (~274 tokens)
- Avg content per active card: **1,076 chars** (~269 tokens)
- Avg content per archived card: **182 chars** (~46 tokens)
- Active cards are content-heavy; archived cards are mostly stubs

## 6. Growth Projections

| Cards | File Size | Raw Tokens | Dump Tokens (est.) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 100 | ~107 KB | ~27,441 | ~1,700 |
| 164 (current) | ~176 KB | ~45,003 | ~2,786 |
| 250 | ~268 KB | ~68,603 | ~4,250 |
| 500 | ~536 KB | ~137,205 | ~8,500 |
| 1,000 | ~1,072 KB | ~274,410 | ~17,000 |

**Scaling concern**: At 500 cards, raw file = ~137K tokens (significant context pressure). At 1,000 cards, raw file exceeds many model context windows. However, `dump` stays manageable (~17K tokens at 1,000 cards).

## 7. Recommendations

1. **Never read cards.json directly** — use `dump` (16x cheaper) or targeted `read`/`find`
2. **Archive aggressively** — archived cards still consume 4.7% of file but provide no active value
3. **Consider purge for archived cards** — removing 46 archived cards would save ~13K bytes
4. **At 500+ cards**, consider multi-file splitting or lazy-load subtrees
5. **CLI overhead is negligible** — 50-88ms per operation, dominated by Node.js startup time

children.title: Landscape: what are people using today?
children.body:
# Agent Memory & Context Landscape Research (March 2026)

## 1. Common Patterns in AI Coding Workflows

### Flat Instruction Files
- **CLAUDE.md**: Claude Code's primary memory file. Loaded at session start, counts toward token budget. Best practice: keep under ~500 lines (~2K tokens). Move specialized content to `.claude/rules/` files or docs/. Supports `@path` imports for on-demand loading. ([Claude Code Docs](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/memory), [HumanLayer Guide](https://www.humanlayer.dev/blog/writing-a-good-claude-md))
- **.cursorrules / .cursor/rules/*.mdc**: Cursor's equivalent. Each rule is a Markdown Component file with glob patterns controlling when it applies. Injected into every AI prompt. ([Cursor Docs](https://docs.cursor.com/context/rules-for-ai))
- **.github/copilot-instructions.md**: GitHub Copilot's repo-level custom instructions. Copilot now also supports agent-specific instruction files for granular per-agent behavior. ([GitHub Docs](https://docs.github.com/copilot/customizing-copilot/adding-custom-instructions-for-github-copilot))
- **Windsurf Rules**: Global, workspace, or system-level rules manually defined by the user. Cascade also auto-generates memories stored in `~/.codeium/windsurf/memories/`. ([Windsurf Docs](https://docs.windsurf.com/windsurf/cascade/memories))
- **Cline .clinerules**: Rules that govern context handoff behavior, allowing Cline to proactively manage context before degradation (e.g., "if context usage > 50%, propose handoff"). ([Cline Blog](https://cline.bot/blog/unlocking-persistent-memory-how-clines-new_task-tool-eliminates-context-window-limitations))

### Structured Directories
- `.planning/`, `.cursor/`, `.claude/rules/`, `.memory/`, `memory-bank/` — teams create structured directories to separate concerns (architecture docs, phase plans, conventions, etc.)
- **Cursor Memory Bank**: Community project with a `.memory` directory at project root. Six phase-based commands (/van, /plan, /creative, /build, /reflect, /archive). Hierarchical rule-loading claims ~70% token reduction vs. loading everything. ([GitHub](https://github.com/vanzan01/cursor-memory-bank), [Lullabot](https://www.lullabot.com/articles/supercharge-your-ai-coding-cursor-rules-and-memory-banks))

### Flat Markdown Files
- TODO.md, ARCHITECTURE.md, DECISIONS.md, etc. — the "denormalized markdown cache" pattern
- Common but prone to state drift: multiple files describing the same thing get out of sync
- No query capability — agents must read entire files to find relevant info

### In-Conversation Memory
- **Claude auto-memory**: Accumulates knowledge across sessions automatically (build commands, debugging insights, style preferences). Stored in user-level config.
- **ChatGPT Memory**: Extracts facts from conversations, persists across sessions. User can view/edit/delete memories.
- **Windsurf Cascade Memories**: Auto-generated during conversation, workspace-scoped, stored locally.
- Limitation: opaque, not version-controlled, not shareable across team members

### MCP Servers for Context
- Emerging as the standard protocol for connecting agents to external context
- MCP donated to Linux Foundation's Agentic AI Foundation (Dec 2025), backed by Google, Microsoft, AWS, Cloudflare, OpenAI
- Memory is becoming a "first-class MCP primitive" in 2026

---

## 2. Existing Tools/Plugins for Agent Memory

### Tier 1: Production-Ready Platforms

| Tool | Approach | Key Differentiator | Pricing |
|------|----------|-------------------|---------|
| **[Mem0](https://mem0.ai)** | Structured summarization + conflict resolution | 26% accuracy gain on memory benchmarks; most integrations; OpenMemory MCP server | Free: 10K memories; $19/mo → $249/mo jump |
| **[Zep](https://getzep.com)** | Temporal Knowledge Graph | 18.5% accuracy gain + 90% latency reduction; tracks how facts change over time | Managed service |
| **[Letta](https://letta.com)** (formerly MemGPT) | LLM-as-OS paradigm; self-editing memory | Core/archival memory hierarchy; agent manages its own context like an OS manages RAM | Open source + managed |
| **[Cognee](https://cognee.ai)** | Knowledge engine pipeline | Outperforms Mem0, Graphiti, LightRAG on HotPotQA benchmarks; combines vector + graph | Open source |

### Tier 2: MCP-Native Memory Servers

| Tool | Approach | Context Cost |
|------|----------|-------------|
| **[OpenMemory](https://mem0.ai/openmemory)** (by Mem0) | Local-first MCP memory server | Moderate; full memory tools exposed |
| **[MemoryGraph](https://memorygraph.dev)** | Graph DB-based MCP memory | **2-3% of context** (vs 20%+ for competitors); 9 core tools |
| **[Hindsight](https://hindsight.vectorize.io)** | Structured fact extraction + entity resolution | Moderate |
| **[ContextStream](https://github.com/contextstream/mcp-server)** | Cloud-based cross-session MCP memory | Cloud-dependent |

### Tier 3: Framework-Integrated Memory

| Tool | Ecosystem | Notes |
|------|-----------|-------|
| **LangMem** | LangGraph | Memory tools integrated with LangGraph workflows; requires self-hosting |
| **Supermemory** | General | Semantic memory at scale with temporal awareness; self-hosted |
| **Graphiti** (by Zep) | General | Open-source real-time knowledge graphs |

### Card/Tree-Based Approaches (Burrow's Niche)
- **No direct competitors found** using a nested card/tree metaphor for agent memory
- **A-Mem** (academic) uses Zettelkasten-like interconnected notes — closest conceptual match, but research-only ([arXiv](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2502.12110))
- **MemOS** offers hierarchical memory mapping with slicing/tagging — academic, not productized ([Paper](https://statics.memtensor.com.cn/files/MemOS_0707.pdf))
- Most tools use flat key-value, graph, or vector approaches — none offer the "infinitely nestable cards" metaphor
- **Burrow's differentiator**: tree-structured, file-based, zero-dependency, git-committable, designed specifically for coding agent workflows

---

## 3. What Power Users Do

### Addy Osmani's Workflow (2026)
- Treats LLM as pair programmer requiring clear direction + context + oversight
- Spawns multiple AI sessions with different models (Claude writes, Gemini reviews)
- Emphasizes specific, contextual instructions over vague requests
- ([Blog Post](https://addyosmani.com/blog/ai-coding-workflow/))

### Common Power User Patterns
- **Context budget awareness**: Power users treat context like a scarce resource. "Give the AI the right information, not just more information." ([Vibehackers](https://vibehackers.io/blog/context-engineering-guide))
- **Session hygiene**: Start new chats for new tasks instead of continuing indefinitely. Use /clear over /compact. ([Claude Code Best Practices](https://cuong.io/blog/2025/06/15-claude-code-best-practices-memory-management))
- **Layered memory**: Keep CLAUDE.md minimal (what's needed every session), put details in docs/, reference with @path
- **Phase-based loading**: Only load context relevant to current phase (Cursor Memory Bank claims 70% token savings)
- **Multi-model workflows**: Use different models for different tasks (coding vs review vs planning)

### 2025 Stack Overflow Finding
- Developers spend an average of **23% of AI interaction time** just providing context that should already be known

---

## 4. Token Costs by Approach

### Estimated Token Consumption Per Session

| Approach | Tokens/Session | Scales? | Notes |
|----------|---------------|---------|-------|
| **CLAUDE.md** (minimal) | 500-2,000 | Yes | One-time load; best kept under 500 lines |
| **CLAUDE.md** (bloated) | 5,000-15,000 | No | Over 200 lines reduces adherence |
| **.cursorrules** (per-file) | 200-1,000 | Yes | Glob-scoped; only loads relevant rules |
| **Flat markdown docs** | 2,000-50,000+ | No | Must read entire files; no query capability |
| **Cursor Memory Bank** | ~30% of typical | Moderate | Phase-based loading helps; still markdown |
| **Burrow cards.json** | 500-5,000 | Yes | Tree structure enables selective loading; CLI queries |
| **MCP memory (MemoryGraph)** | **2-3% of context** | Yes | Best-in-class efficiency; graph queries |
| **MCP memory (others)** | 10-20%+ of context | Moderate | Tool definitions + retrieved memories |
| **Letta/MemGPT** | Variable | Yes | Agent self-manages; core memory always in context |
| **Vector DB RAG** | 2,000-10,000 | Yes | Retrieved chunks only; but retrieval quality varies |
| **Full codebase read** | 50,000-500,000+ | No | 2x pricing above 200K tokens on Claude |

### Claude Code Specific Costs
- Average: **$6/dev/day**, 90th percentile under $12/day
- Monthly: ~$100-200/dev with Sonnet 4.6
- Above 200K tokens: **2x input pricing, 1.5x output pricing**
- Opus 4.6 scores 76% on MRCR at 1M tokens; Sonnet 4.5 scores 18.5% — Opus is dramatically better for long context

---

## 5. Novel Approaches & Academic Research

### Key Papers

1. **"Memory in the Age of AI Agents: A Survey"** (Dec 2025, 47 authors) — Comprehensive taxonomy across three axes: Form (token/parametric/latent), Function (factual/experiential/working), Dynamics (formation/evolution/retrieval). ([arXiv:2512.13564](https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.13564), [Paper List](https://github.com/Shichun-Liu/Agent-Memory-Paper-List))

2. **A-Mem: Agentic Memory for LLM Agents** — Zettelkasten-inspired system where agent dynamically organizes memories into interconnected knowledge networks through indexing and linking. ([arXiv:2502.12110](https://arxiv.org/abs/2502.12110))

3. **MemOS: A Memory OS for AI Systems** — Memory slicing, tagging, hierarchical mapping, and context binding. Treats memory as an OS-level concern. ([Paper](https://statics.memtensor.com.cn/files/MemOS_0707.pdf))

4. **Mem0 Research Paper** — Structured summarization achieving 26% accuracy gain on standard benchmarks while reducing token costs. ([arXiv:2504.19413](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2504.19413))

### Emerging Research Frontiers (from survey)
- Memory automation (agents that learn to manage their own memory)
- RL integration for memory policies
- Multimodal memory (images, audio, not just text)
- Multi-agent shared memory
- Trustworthiness/privacy of agent memory

### Novel Open Source Approaches
- **Beads**: Hybrid Git+SQLite architecture for agent state persistence. Agents maintain state across long-running sessions with failure recovery. ([Blog](https://dasroot.net/posts/2026/02/building-persistent-memory-ai-agents-go-beads/))
- **ByteRover**: Agentic memory management platform for workflow automation. ([byterover.dev](https://www.byterover.dev/blog/the-future-of-workflows-why-ai-automation-is-the-standard))
- **Cascade Memory Bank**: Windsurf-specific memory system that documents decisions, progress, and architectural evolution. ([GitHub](https://github.com/GreatScottyMac/cascade-memory-bank))

### Context Engineering as a Discipline
- Anthropic published an official guide: "Effective Context Engineering for AI Agents" — treats context window as scarce resource, designs everything around it. ([Anthropic](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/effective-context-engineering-for-ai-agents))
- Google's approach: context as "compiled view over richer stateful system" — sessions, memory, artifacts are structured state; flows/processors compile it into working context.
