- .claude/burrow/lib/mongoose.cjs
- .claude/burrow/lib/warren.cjs
- .claude/burrow/lib/render.cjs
- .claude/burrow/workflows/burrow.md

SLASH COMMANDS (copied verbatim):
- .claude/commands/burrow.md (top-level /burrow command)
- .claude/commands/burrow/add.md, read.md, edit.md, move.md, remove.md, archive.md, unarchive.md, dump.md, help.md

All copied files are read-only from the user's perspective — they should not edit them. Updates replace them wholesale.

children.title: Generated at install time (not in package)
children.body:
These files are created fresh by the installer, never copied from the package:

- .planning/burrow/cards.json — empty v2 schema: {"version": 2, "cards": []}. Only created if it doesn't already exist (idempotent). This is USER DATA — never overwritten on update.
- CLAUDE.md — the 'Burrow — Agent Memory' section is appended (or file created). Checked by searching for the sentinel string. Also user-owned — installer appends but never removes.
- .gitignore entry for cards.json.bak — should be added by installer (currently a known TODO, card 3dad744a). Backup files are write artifacts, not versionable.

Key principle: generated files belong to the user. The installer creates them once, then hands off ownership. Updates must never touch cards.json or remove the CLAUDE.md section.

children.title: What must NOT be installed
children.body:
These exist in the repo but must never reach the target project:

- test/ directory (warren.test.cjs, cli.test.cjs, mongoose.test.cjs, render.test.cjs) — development only
- install.cjs itself — it's the delivery vehicle, not the product
- package.json — belongs to the npm package, not the installed product
- README.md, LICENSE — package metadata, not runtime files
- .planning/ — burrow's own project data, not the user's
- field-reports/, docs, .claude/commands that aren't burrow commands
- Any dotfiles (.npmignore, .gitignore from the burrow repo)
- cards.json.bak — write artifact, never distributed

The .npmignore or package.json 'files' field enforces what enters the tarball. The installer's copyDirSync controls what reaches the target. Both gates matter — belt and suspenders.

children.title: Version marker strategy
children.body:
A version marker should be installed so the update mechanism can detect what's present. Recommended approach:

FILE: .claude/burrow/VERSION (plain text, single line, e.g. '1.2.0')

Why a separate file, not package.json:
- package.json doesn't get installed (it's package metadata)
- Embedding version in burrow-tools.cjs mixes concerns
- A standalone VERSION file is trivially readable by both the installer (fs.readFileSync) and the agent (cat)
- Easy to compare: read local VERSION, compare to package version from npm registry

The installer writes this file on every install/update. The update mechanism reads it to decide whether to proceed. The agent can read it for --version output.

Alternative considered: JSON file with version + install date. Overkill for now — a plain semver string is sufficient. Can evolve later if needed.

children.title: Separation of concerns: package vs product
children.body:
The boundary is crisp along two axes:

PACKAGE (ephemeral delivery vehicle):
- Lives in npm registry or GitHub release
- Contains installer + source + commands + metadata
- Run via npx — downloaded, executed, discarded
- Never a runtime dependency — no node_modules entry in target project
- User never sees or interacts with package contents directly

PRODUCT (persistent, lives in the project):
- .claude/burrow/ — source code (replaceable on update)
- .claude/commands/burrow* — slash commands (replaceable on update)
- .planning/burrow/cards.json — user data (NEVER replaced)
- CLAUDE.md section — user config (append-only)
- .claude/burrow/VERSION — version stamp (replaced on update)

UPDATE RULE: files split into two categories:
1. REPLACEABLE: source, commands, VERSION, workflow. Overwritten on every update.
2. SACRED: cards.json, CLAUDE.md section. Created once, never touched again.

The installer must know which category each file belongs to. Current install.cjs skips existing files — this needs to change for updates. Replaceable files should always be overwritten; sacred files should always be preserved.

children.title: Current installer gaps to fix
children.body:
The existing install.cjs has issues that need addressing for proper packaging:

1. SKIP-IF-EXISTS BUG: copyDirSync skips if burrow-tools.cjs exists. This blocks updates — replaceable files should always be overwritten. Fix: always copy source and commands; only skip cards.json and CLAUDE.md section.

2. NO EXCLUSION FILTER: copyDirSync copies everything recursively. If tests or other dev files end up in .claude/burrow/, they get copied. Fix: either (a) use a whitelist of files to copy, or (b) ensure the package tarball only contains installable files so blind copy is safe.

3. NO VERSION STAMP: doesn't write a VERSION file. Can't detect installed version.

4. NO .gitignore ENTRY: doesn't add cards.json.bak to .gitignore (known TODO, card 3dad744a).

5. PATH ASSUMPTIONS: hardcoded to read from __dirname/.claude/burrow/ — assumes running from the burrow repo root. For npm packaging, paths must resolve relative to the package installation, not a git clone.

6. NO PERMISSIONS: doesn't set file permissions. burrow-tools.cjs should arguably be executable (chmod +x) for direct invocation, though 'node burrow-tools.cjs' works without it.

children.title: v1.3 Burrow-as-Project-Backbone
children.body:
## Vision

Use Burrow to manage the Burrow project itself — replace GSD's .planning/ markdown files with Burrow cards. The repo becomes a live demo of Burrow's capabilities.

## Why

1. Eat your own dog food — prove Burrow works at real project scale
2. The repo IS the advertisement — anyone who clones it sees Burrow in action
3. Eliminate the .planning/ drift problem we've been documenting

## Scope

~897 KB / ~224K tokens of GSD planning content to migrate. Milestones dir alone is 593 KB. Completed milestones get archived (invisible by default).

## Prerequisite

Phase 1 must ship the `tree` command and context control settings before migration begins. Without cheap loading, the experiment fails at this data volume (~1.1 MB cards.json post-migration).

## Strategy

Phase 1: Build scaffolding (tree command, context control, settings)
Phase 2+: Migrate GSD content into Burrow, run the project through it

children.title: Phase 1: Map command & context control
children.body:
## Goal

Ship the `map` command and a settings system that controls how the agent loads Burrow on session start. This is scaffolding — must land before the migration experiment begins.

## Deliverables

### 1. `burrow map` command
- Outputs nested card titles and IDs only — no bodies, no metadata, no chrome
- Supports `--depth N` flag to limit how deep
- Supports `--include-archived` flag
- Output format: indented, minimal, agent-friendly
- Target: ~30-50 lines of code

### 2. Context control settings
A persistent config (e.g., `.claude/burrow/config.json` or similar) with a `loadMode` setting:

- **`auto`** (sensible default): Burrow picks based on cards.json size. Small burrows (<X KB) get full load via `dump`. Large burrows get `map` only.
- **`full`** (yolo mode): Always load everything on session start via `dump`. For users who want maximum context and don't care about token cost.
- **`map`** (controlled mode): Only load the map on session start. Agent drills down on demand. For power users with large burrows.
- **`map --depth N`**: Like map but capped at depth N. Maximum control.
- **`none`**: Load nothing on session start. Agent must explicitly request what it needs. Maximum control for scripted/specialized agents.

### 3. Updated workflow instructions
Rewrite `.claude/burrow/workflows/burrow.md` Step 1 (LOAD) to respect the loadMode setting instead of always reading cards.json directly.

## Open Questions
- What's the size threshold for `auto` mode to switch from full to map? (50KB? 100KB? Based on research: ~10K tokens / ~40KB is the comfort zone)
- Where does the config live? Alongside cards.json? In .claude/burrow/

children.title: map command: lightweight index view
children.body:
A new command that outputs just nested card titles and IDs — no bodies, no metadata, no chrome. The agent runs this on session start to see what's in the burrow, then uses read <id> to drill into anything it needs.

Command name: `map` (as in: show me the map of the burrow)

Output would look something like:

[96b432c7] Milestones
  [0155be04] v1.1
    [4fb8cfc8] Eliminate flatten-then-renest pipeline
    [8a01bb38] Improve tree alignment at deep nesting levels
[a55b651e] CLI
  [988b4781] Batch operations for commands

Purpose: replace the current workflow of reading raw cards.json (~45k tokens) with a cheap map (~1-2k tokens) that lets the agent discover and probe incrementally.

Supports --depth flag to limit how deep the map goes. Default: full depth.

children.title: Batch operations for commands
children.body:
Support multiple card IDs in a single command for commands that take no extra positional arguments:

- `burrow read id1 id2 id3` — show all three cards
- `burrow remove id1 id2 id3` — delete all three (and their children)
- `burrow archive id1 id2 id3` — archive all three (and their children)
- `burrow unarchive id1 id2 id3` — restore all three (and their children)

Cascade behavior unchanged: each ID applies to all its children.

## Why only these four

Every other command (`add`, `edit`, `move`) has additional arguments (--title, --body, --to, --at) that make multi-ID ambiguous. These four take only target IDs.

## Confirmation behavior

- `read`: no confirmation (read-only)
- `remove`: batched confirmation: "Remove 'X' (3 children), 'Y' (1 child), and 'Z'? [3 cards + 4 descendants]"
- `archive`/`unarchive`: no confirmation (reversible)

## Previous workaround

Chaining commands with `&&` (see child card). Multi-ID makes this native.

children.title: Trick: chain commands with &&
children.body:
Multiple CLI mutations can be chained in a single Bash call using &&. The agent already does this naturally for multi-step operations (e.g., moving 7 cards in one shot). This gives batch-like behavior without needing a dedicated batch command.

Example — move 7 cards in one call:
```
node .claude/burrow/burrow-tools.cjs move bf82ebbc --to d607a750 && \
node .claude/burrow/burrow-tools.cjs move 0c6a5c42 --to d607a750 && \
node .claude/burrow/burrow-tools.cjs move 0da4078b --to d607a750 && \
...
```

Example — create 6 research cards in one call:
```
node .claude/burrow/burrow-tools.cjs add --title "Q1" --parent d0d47a24 --body "..." && \
node .claude/burrow/burrow-tools.cjs add --title "Q2" --parent d0d47a24 --body "..." && \
...
```

Trade-off: if any command fails, subsequent commands don't run (&&). Use ; instead of && if you want all commands to attempt regardless of failures.

children.title: Phase 2: GSD-to-Burrow migration experiment
children.body:
## Goal

Migrate the Burrow project's own GSD planning content into Burrow cards. Run the project through Burrow instead of .planning/ markdown files. The repo becomes the product demo.

## Depends on
Phase 1 (tree command + context control) must ship first.

## Critical: GSD Internal Component Surgery

GSD agents (planner, executor, verifier, researcher, roadmapper) have hardcoded file paths and conventions baked into their prompts and tooling. We cannot just swap the storage — we need to surgically replace the specific GSD components that read/write .planning/ files so they read/write Burrow cards instead. This requires deep research into GSD internals BEFORE any migration begins.

Research must cover:
- Which GSD agents touch which files (mapping of agent → file dependencies)
- Which components can be swapped with minimal blast radius
- Which components are too entangled to touch (leave as-is, adapt around them)
- Whether we patch GSD locally or build a shim layer
- Risk of breaking GSD's own guarantees (atomic commits, state tracking, verification)

Get this wrong and we break the workflow. Get it right and Burrow becomes a drop-in planning backend.

## What gets migrated
- Core files: PROJECT.md, ROADMAP.md, STATE.md, REQUIREMENTS.md → cards
- Research docs → cards under a Research parent
- Phase plans and summaries → cards under milestone cards
- Completed milestones → archived milestone cards

## What stays as-is
- .planning/burrow/ (that's Burrow itself)
- Code, tests, CLI — no changes

## What we're measuring
- Does the workflow actually work? Can we plan, execute, and track phases entirely through Burrow?
- Token cost per session compared to GSD's .planning/ approach
- Drift: does state stay consistent across sessions?
- Pain points: what's missing, what's awkward?

## Success criteria
- The project runs on Burrow for at least one full milestone cycle (plan → execute → verify → archive)
- The repo README can point to the card tree as a live demo

children.title: v1.2.3 Onboarding & Configuration
children.body:
## Vision

Give users control over how Burrow behaves — starting from install and adjustable anytime after. The installer becomes a proper onboarding flow that asks meaningful questions, and a new /burrow:config command lets users change their mind later.

## Why

The current installer copies files and optionally appends to CLAUDE.md — that's it. There are no choices about how Burrow runs. Meanwhile, the biggest operational cost is the session-start load: the agent reads the full cards.json (~30K tokens at 157 cards), whether the session needs it or not. Users should decide how much context they want loaded, and the installer should walk them through it.

## Key Changes

1. **`burrow index` command** — lightweight tree output (titles + IDs only, no bodies) for cheap session starts
2. **Config system** — persistent settings in `.claude/burrow/config.json`, respected by the workflow
3. **Installer onboarding prompts** — ask about context loading mode during install
4. **/burrow:config command** — view and change settings from within Claude Code
5. **Updated CLAUDE.md snippet** — sentinel block reflects the chosen loading mode
6. **Updated workflow** — Step 1 (LOAD) respects loadMode setting instead of always reading full cards.json

## Build Order (dependencies)

Phase 1: index command + config system (the primitives — no dependencies)
Phase 2: workflow update + CLAUDE.md snippet variants (wires config into agent behavior — depends on Phase 1)
Phase 3: installer onboarding + /burrow:config command (user-facing surfaces — depends on Phases 1-2)

## Context Loading Modes

- **full** (current default): Read entire cards.json on session start. Best for small burrows.
- **index**: Run `burrow index` on session start — titles, IDs, structure only (~95% token reduction). Agent drills down with `read <id>` as needed.
- **index --depth N**: Index capped at depth N. Maximum control.
- **none**: Load nothing on session start. Agent requests what it needs explicitly.
- **auto**: Burrow picks based on cards.json size. Small = full, large = index.

## Related Research

- Selective loading strategies: card 9c6426ca (detailed token savings analysis)
- Token cost analysis: card 7a5f1dd1 (cards.json vs GSD comparison)
- v1.3 map command plans: card 5784bfd9 (earlier design notes)

## Scope

IN: Config system, index command, installer onboarding, /burrow:config command, workflow update, CLAUDE.md snippet variants.
OUT: Multi-file sharding, subtree extraction, GSD migration, plugin packaging.

children.title: burrow index command
children.body:
New CLI command that outputs a lightweight tree — titles, IDs, child counts, and archive status only. No bodies, no ages, no box-drawing chrome. JSON output meant for agent consumption on session start.

Flags:
- --depth N (limit depth, default unlimited)
- --include-archived
- --flat (flat array with parentId instead of nested)

Token savings (from research card 9c6426ca):
- Full cards.json today: ~30K tokens
- index (active only): ~6,200 tokens (79% reduction)
- index --depth 2: ~1,600 tokens (95% reduction)

Implementation: ~50 lines in burrow-tools.cjs, ~20 lines helper in mongoose.cjs. Recursive tree walk that strips body fields and outputs clean JSON.

children.title: Config system (config.json)
children.body:
Persistent configuration stored at .claude/burrow/config.json. Read by the CLI and workflow on every invocation.

Settings:
- loadMode: 'full' | 'index' | 'index --depth N' | 'none' | 'auto' (default: 'auto')
- autoThreshold: size in KB at which auto mode switches from full to index (default: 50)

Implementation:
- New lib/config.cjs module — load/save/get/set functions
- Default config created during install
- Config file is SACRED (never overwritten on update, like cards.json)
- Zero dependencies — just fs read/write of JSON

children.title: Installer onboarding prompts
children.body:
Expand the installer's interactive flow to ask about context loading during setup.

New prompt (after CLAUDE.md opt-in):
- 'How should Burrow load on session start?' with options:
  - Auto (recommended) — picks based on size
  - Full — always load everything
  - Index only — titles and structure, drill down on demand
  - None — load nothing, ask explicitly

The chosen mode is written to config.json and the CLAUDE.md sentinel snippet is adjusted to match. On upgrade, existing config is preserved (SACRED file).

Also: the --yes flag should pick 'auto' as the default.

children.title: /burrow:config command
children.body:
New slash command: /burrow:config — view and change Burrow settings from within Claude Code.

Usage:
- /burrow:config — show all current settings
- /burrow:config loadMode index — change the loading mode
- /burrow:config autoThreshold 100 — change the auto threshold

Implementation:
- New .claude/commands/burrow/config.md command file
- CLI: 'node burrow-tools.cjs config [key] [value]'
  - No args: print all settings as pretty table
  - One arg: print that setting's value
  - Two args: set the value, print confirmation
- After changing loadMode, prompt: 'Update CLAUDE.md to match? (y/n)' — rewrites the sentinel block with the new loading instructions.

children.title: CLAUDE.md snippet variants
children.body:
The sentinel block in CLAUDE.md currently hardcodes 'read cards.json using the Read tool'. It needs to vary based on loadMode.

Variants:
- full: 'silently read .planning/burrow/cards.json using the Read tool' (current behavior)
- index: 'run node .claude/burrow/burrow-tools.cjs index to load the card tree structure, then read specific cards as needed'
- index --depth N: same as index but with --depth flag
- none: 'Burrow is available but not pre-loaded. Use /burrow commands to interact.'
- auto: 'run node .claude/burrow/burrow-tools.cjs load to let Burrow decide what to load based on data size'

The installer writes the correct variant. /burrow:config rewrites it when loadMode changes.

children.title: Workflow update (LOAD step)
children.body:
Rewrite Step 1 (LOAD) in .claude/burrow/workflows/burrow.md to respect the loadMode config.

Current behavior: always 'Read cards.json using the Read tool'.

New behavior:
1. Read .claude/burrow/config.json to get loadMode
2. Based on mode:
   - full: Read cards.json via Read tool (current)
   - index: Run 'burrow index' via Bash, parse JSON into memory
   - index --depth N: Run 'burrow index --depth N'
   - none: Skip load entirely, note that cards are available on demand
   - auto: Check cards.json size, pick full or index accordingly
3. On any /burrow command, if only index was loaded and the command needs body content, the agent knows to run 'read <id> --full' for specific cards.

The workflow should also document the 'lazy body fetching' pattern: load index first, drill down as needed.

cards.title: Claude Code plugin distribution
cards.body:
Package burrow as a native Claude Code plugin for marketplace distribution. Users would install via /plugin install from within Claude Code — zero friction, native slash commands, marketplace discoverability (75+ plugins already listed).

ORIGIN: v1.2 research agents discovered this during distribution mechanism evaluation. See cards under [2f0389b7] v1.2 Packaging & Distribution:
- [067b5f31] Option 6: Claude Code plugin system — full pros/cons analysis
- [52a034a5] Option D: Plugin distribution — scope/install tradeoffs
- [49a64048] RECOMMENDATION card — plugin-first was the original recommendation before we chose npx

KEY QUESTIONS TO RESOLVE:
- Can a plugin handle scaffolding? (creating .planning/burrow/cards.json, appending to CLAUDE.md)
- What is the .claude-plugin/plugin.json manifest format?
- Do we need to convert commands/ to skills/ format?
- How do plugins handle per-project data initialization?
- What's the Anthropic marketplace submission process?

WHY DEFERRED: npx create-burrow is concrete, works today, zero dependency on plugin API stability. Plugin system is newer — worth waiting for it to mature before committing. But this is the endgame for distribution — same audience, native integration, marketplace discovery.

cards.title: npm publish workflow
cards.body:
1. Commit changes first (npm version requires clean tree)
2. npm version patch|minor|major (bumps package.json, creates commit + git tag)
3. npm publish (prepublishOnly syncs VERSION automatically)
4. git push && git push --tags

Key rules:
- Can't republish a version — if you mess up, bump again
- npm pack to inspect tarball locally before publishing
- Use --tag beta for pre-releases
- files in package.json is the allowlist for what ships

cards.title: Project Guidelines
cards.body:
How to use Burrow for managing this project. Read this card when starting a new milestone or onboarding a new agent session.

## Milestone Cards

When planning a new milestone, build the full context as a Burrow card tree under Milestones so any agent can read it and start working.

A milestone card should contain:
- **Vision** — what and why, in 2-3 sentences
- **Why** — the problem being solved, with concrete data if available
- **Key Changes** — numbered list of deliverables
- **Build Order** — phase dependencies (what must ship before what)
- **Context Loading Modes / Key Concepts** — define any new concepts introduced
- **Related Research** — card IDs pointing to prior research or backlog analysis
- **Scope** — explicit IN/OUT boundaries

Each deliverable gets a **child card** with:
- What it does (one paragraph)
- API/CLI design (flags, usage examples)
- Implementation notes (estimated complexity, which files change)
- Data from research cards where applicable (token savings, benchmarks, etc.)

The goal: an agent running `read <milestone-id> --depth 0 --full` gets everything it needs to create a GSD milestone, plan phases, and start building — no additional context gathering required.

## Research Cards

Store research findings as cards under the relevant milestone or backlog item. Include measured data (token counts, file sizes, benchmarks) — not just opinions. Reference research cards by ID from milestone bodies.

## Backlog

Future ideas live under the Backlog card. When promoting a backlog item to a milestone, reference the original card ID so the research carries forward.

cards.title: Milestone Closing Checklist
cards.body:
Run through before every npm publish or milestone close:

1. Version bump — package.json version matches the tag being created
2. .gitignore — no runtime/generated files leaking into git
3. README — all CLI commands documented in both the CLI Reference and shortcut commands list
4. Slash commands — every user-facing CLI command has a .md file in .claude/commands/burrow/
5. Workflow reference table — workflows/burrow.md command table lists all commands
6. Help command — /burrow:help lists all available commands with examples
7. Tests — every CLI command has test coverage in test/cli.test.cjs
8. CLAUDE.md — accurately describes current project state
9. No stale references — grep for old names, removed features, dead imports
10. git status clean — no uncommitted changes that should be included

cards.title: Design principle: transparency and consent
cards.body: Silent behavior is fine — what matters is that the user was told it would happen and had the chance to consent or opt out. Every feature that changes how Claude behaves must: (1) be clearly communicated during installation, (2) give the user a way to turn it off, (3) never surprise the user with something they didn't know was happening. It's not about avoiding automation — it's about managing expectations and respecting the user's agency over their own machine.

cards.title: Version bump checklist
cards.body: When bumping version: (1) VERSION file, (2) package.json via npm version X.Y.Z --no-git-tag-version. Both must be updated together.

cards.title: Only tag/version on explicit approval
cards.body: Do not create git tags or bump versions automatically. Multiple commits can go into one version. Always ask before tagging or bumping.
